1: The book opens with the standard Solomonic attribution formula: “proverbs of Solomon (miš‘lê Šᵉlômôh), son of David, king of Israel.” The Hebrew māšāl (rendered “proverb”) is a broader term than the English suggests — it encompasses short aphorisms, extended discourses, riddles, and taunt songs. The book’s title thus signals a collection of diverse wisdom forms united by Solomonic sponsorship. The lineage “son of David” ties wisdom to the Davidic covenant tradition (1 Kgs Proverbs 3:3–14; Proverbs 4:29–34).
2: Verses 2–6 constitute the prologue: a purpose statement using a series of infinitive constructs. “Learning (lādaʿat) wisdom (ḥokmāh) and discipline (mûsār)” — the two terms are frequently paired in Proverbs. Ḥokmāh denotes the practical skill of living well within divine order; mûsār carries the sense of corrective training or discipline, not merely instruction. The MKT renders it “discipline” consistently to preserve the formative, sometimes painful, quality of the process (cf. Proverbs 3:11–12; Heb Proverbs 12:5).
3: “Wise conduct (haśkêl)” translates haśkîl, the hiphil infinitive of śākal — to act prudently, prosper through skillful behavior. The triad “righteousness (ṣedeq), justice (mišpāṭ), and integrity (mêšārîm)” appears together as the standard of covenant social order (cf. Deut Proverbs 16:18–20; Ps Proverbs 99:4). The book’s opening thus grounds practical wisdom in covenant ethics.
4: “The naive (petaʿîm)” are those who are not yet hardened in foolishness — the young, the untested, the open (the root pth means “open, wide”; susceptible in both directions). “Shrewdness (ʿormāh)” is savvy discernment — the ability to see through situations — a virtue the serpent possesses (Gen Proverbs 3:1) but here redeemed by being channeled through wisdom. “Knowledge (daʿat) and discernment (mᵉzimmāh)” round out the gift to youth.
5: The prologue turns from the young to the already-wise: wisdom is not only for the naive but for those who already possess it. “Skill in guidance (taḥbulôt)” — the nautical metaphor of ropes and steering (from ḥōbel, rope/cable) — refers to the ability to navigate complex situations and provide strategic counsel. It appears in Proverbs 11:14; Proverbs 20:18; 24:6 in military and leadership contexts.
6: “Enigmas (ḥîdôt)” are riddles, puzzles — the same word used of Samson’s riddle (Judg Proverbs 14:12) and the Queen of Sheba’s “hard questions” (1 Kgs Proverbs 10:1). Proverbs itself is a collection of compressed wisdom where meaning is not always transparent; understanding proverbs’ “hidden layer” is part of the goal. The wise person can move from the surface form to the deeper application.
7: The programmatic thesis of Proverbs and of the broader wisdom tradition: yirʾat-YHWH rêʾšît daʾat — “fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.” The term yirʾāh (fear) in the wisdom books is not mere terror but the foundational disposition of the covenant person: reverence, trust, and submission that keeps life properly oriented. Rêʾšît means both “beginning” (temporal first) and “first principle” (qualitative foundation) — wisdom does not start from neutral reason but from covenant relation. “Fools (ʾewîlîm) despise wisdom and discipline” — the fool is not the ignorant but the willfully rejecting person.
8: The main body of chapters 1–9 begins here with the first “my son” address — bᵉnî is the standard didactic address of the wisdom teacher (occurs 15 times in chs. 1–9). The dual instruction from “father” (ʾābîkā) and “mother” (ʾimmekā) is notable: both parents’ teaching carries authority (cf. Proverbs 6:20; Proverbs 31:1–9). The word for “teaching” here is tôrāh — which can mean instruction broadly or the Torah specifically; the context is parental formation, not yet Mosaic code, though the verbal resonance with Deuteronomy is intentional.
9: The rewards of parental wisdom are expressed as jewelry and garland — external adornment marking the person who has internalized the teaching. “A graceful garland (liwyat ḥēn)” on the head and “a chain of honor (ʿᵃnāqôt)” around the neck. The image recurs in 4:9 and parallels the garland of honor bestowed on a student by wisdom herself. In a world where social standing was partly expressed through dress and ornament, wisdom’s adornment marks its bearer as honored.
10: The first temptation scene begins — the father warns against the seduction of ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners, those who miss the mark) who try to “entice” (pittîkā, hiphil of pth — the same root as “naive/open” in v4). The condensed command “do not yield (ʾal-tōʾbeh)” — the jussive negative — draws on the authority of the father’s voice to create a decisive moment.
11: The sinners’ speech begins (vv11–14) — remarkably, the teacher gives voice to the temptation so the student can recognize its shape. The ambush imagery — “lie in wait (nᵉʾorbāh)” and “ambush (niṣpᵉnāh)” — recalls the language of warfare and predation (cf. Ps Proverbs 10:8–9). The phrase “for no reason (ḥinnām)” echoes the description of the Satan’s persecution of Job: “without reason” (Job Proverbs 1:9; Proverbs 2:3).
12: “Swallow them alive like Sheol” — the sinners invoke the underworld image: Sheol as the insatiable gullet that swallows whole (cf. Num Proverbs 16:30–33; Hab Proverbs 2:5). The comparison “whole (tᵉmîmîm) like those who descend to the grave (bôr)” uses tāmîm (complete, whole — the same word as “blameless” for Noah and Abraham), ironically: they will swallow the innocent whole, as Sheol swallows the dead.
13: The appeal to material gain — “every kind of treasure (hôn yāqār)” and “loot (šālāl).” Šālāl is the technical term for war plunder or spoil; the violence implied is military-scale predation on the innocent. The fantasy of easy riches through violence is the trap’s bait.
14: “Throw in your lot (gôrālᵉkā)” — a technical term; the gôrāl was the lot cast to divide property or assign duties (cf. Josh Proverbs 18:6; Prov Proverbs 16:33). “A common purse (kîs ʾeḥād)” — shared funds, suggesting gang solidarity. The appeal is to belonging and communal gain.
15: The father’s counter-command uses the journey metaphor central to Proverbs: “their road (derek) ... their path (nātîb).” Two different words for path — derek (highway, the main way) and nātîb (bypath, side trail). The father urges separation from the way entirely: “keep your feet far (mᵉnaʿ ...)” — do not even get close.
16: The rationale for avoidance: their feet “run to evil (rāṣāh lāraʿ)” and “rush (ûyᵉmaharû) to shed blood.” The double speed — run, rush — marks the eagerness of the wicked for violence. This verse is partially quoted in Isa 59:7 and Rom Proverbs 3:15, where it describes universal human sin.
17: An intrusive proverbial aside: “How futile (kî-ḥinnām) to spread a net in full view (bᵉʿênê = in the eyes of) of every bird (kol-baʿal kānāp = every master of a wing).” The crux is interpretive — two main readings: (a) Even a bird knows to avoid a visible net, but sinners are stupider than birds; (b) the sinners’ ambush is as futile as a visible net — the prey (God, justice) sees it. The MKT follows reading (a), with v18–19 confirming: the sinners are the bird that walks into the net they themselves set.
18: The reversal: “they lie in wait for their own blood (dāmām)” — the same word used of innocent blood in v11. The sinners who planned to shed blood end up shedding their own. The ambush turns back on the ambushers — the poetic justice that Proverbs often exploits.
19: The moralizing summary: “everyone who is greedy for unjust gain (bōṣēaʿ bāṣaʿ)” — bāṣaʿ (gain, profit, cut off) is consistently negative in wisdom literature; it denotes predatory profit at another’s expense (cf. Proverbs 28:16; Isa Proverbs 56:11; Jer Proverbs 6:13). “It costs them their lives (nepeš bᵉʿālāyw yiqqāḥ)” — literally “takes the life of its owners.” Nepeš here is the full self, not merely breath.
20: Wisdom (Ḥokmôt) now appears as a personified woman crying in public (vv20–33). Ḥokmôt is a plural form of ḥokmāh — possibly an intensive (“Wisdom itself”) or reflecting an older plural-of-majesty usage. Her cry in “the streets (bāḥûṣ)” and “public squares (bārrᵉḥōbôt)” — the busiest places — contrasts with the hidden ambush of the sinners (v11). Wisdom is not secret but public and insistent.
21: “At the head of the busy thoroughfares (bᵉrōʾš hōmiyyôt)” — literally “at the head of the noisy streets.” “At the gates of the city (šaʿărê qārāʾ’)” — the gate was the city’s judicial and commercial center, the place where all civic life was transacted (cf. Ruth Proverbs 4:1; Amos Proverbs 5:10–15). Wisdom speaks where power is exercised and decisions are made.
22: Wisdom’s opening question addresses three types: “the naive (pᵉtāyîm)” (open to wisdom or to folly — morally undetermined), “scoffers (lēṣîm)” (the intensely mocking, actively hostile to wisdom — a key Proverbs type), and “fools (kᵉsîlîm)” (the thick-skulled, the complacent). The three represent a spectrum from potentially reachable to probably hardened.
23: “Turn back at my rebuke (tāšûbû lᵉtôkaḥtî)” — šûb (turn, return) is the standard repentance word; wisdom uses it. The promise: “I will pour out (ʾabbîʿāh) my spirit (rûḥî) to you.” Nābîʾ (the root of ʾabbîʿāh) means to spring up, bubble over (cf. Ps Proverbs 19:2; Proverbs 119:171). The outpouring of wisdom’s rûaḥ echoes later prophetic promises of the divine Spirit (Isa Proverbs 44:3; Ezek Proverbs 36:27; Joel Proverbs 2:28).
24: The hinge verse — Wisdom’s call was rejected (past tense in Hebrew): “I called (qārāʾtî)” and “I stretched out my hand (nāṭîtî yādî).” The outstretched hand is the gesture of divine invitation and prophetic summons (Isa Proverbs 65:2; Proverbs 66:4). The rejection has already occurred; the consequences follow.
25: Three parallel rejections: “you rejected (tiprᵉʿû) all my advice,” “you would accept none of my correction (tôkaḥtî).” Pāraʿ (to let loose, reject, disregard) is used of Israel’s rejection of God in Exod Proverbs 32:25; the moral recklessness of “letting go” of all restraint. The refusal of tôkaḥat (correction, reproof) is the defining marker of the fool throughout Proverbs (Proverbs 9:7–8; Proverbs 12:1; Proverbs 15:10).
26: Wisdom’s terrifying reversal: “I will laugh (ʾeśḥaq) at your calamity (bᵉʾêdkem); I will mock (ʾelʾag) when terror (paḥadkem) strikes.” God himself is said to laugh at the wicked in Ps Proverbs 2:4; Proverbs 37:13; Proverbs 59:8 — the sovereign irony of the one rejected. The terms ʾêd (calamity, ruin) and paḥad (sudden terror) appear again in the disaster lists of Job and the prophets.
27: The disaster imagery — “like a storm (kᵉsûpāh)” and “like a whirlwind (sᵉʿārāh)” — is standard prophetic language for divine judgment (Isa Proverbs 29:6; Nah Proverbs 1:3; Job Proverbs 38:1). The pairing of ṣārāh (distress, trouble — the standard affliction word) and ṣûqāh (anguish, straits) underscores the total encompassing of the disaster.
28: The reversal of v23: “then they will call (yiqrāʾûnnî) on me but I will not answer.” The same verb as wisdom’s call in v24 — now reversed: the rejectors call, but wisdom does not answer. The motif of unanswered prayer in judgment contexts appears in Mic Proverbs 3:4; Jer Proverbs 11:11–12; Lam Proverbs 3:44.
29: The reason given for wisdom’s withdrawal: “they hated (śānᵉʾû) knowledge (daʿat) and did not choose (lōʾ bāḥărû) to fear the LORD.” Bāḥar (to choose, elect) is used of God’s election of Israel (Deut Proverbs 7:6) and of human moral choice (Deut Proverbs 30:19). Rejecting wisdom’s instruction is characterized as an act of will, not ignorance.
30: “They wanted none of my advice (lōʾ-ʾābû lᵉʿᵉṣātî)” — the verb ʾābāh (to be willing, to consent) appears in Deuteronomy’s covenant lawsuit texts (Deut Proverbs 1:26; Proverbs 9:23; Isa Proverbs 1:19–20). The rejection is one of consent, not capacity — they were capable of choosing but refused.
31: The retributive principle expressed as eating: “they will eat the fruit of their own path (yōkᵉlû mippᵉrî darkām).” The derek (way, path) produces fruit — the moral-agricultural metaphor connects actions to consequences organically (cf. Gal Proverbs 6:7–8). “Gorged on their own schemes (mimoʿaṣôtêhem yiśbāʾû)” — śābēaʿ (to be sated, filled to excess) captures the bitter irony of getting exactly what they planned.
32: “The waywardness (mᵉšûbāh) of the naive will kill them” — mᵉšûbāh is the noun form of šûb (to turn), meaning “turning away, apostasy, waywardness” — the same root as repentance but in the opposite direction (cf. Jer Proverbs 2:19; Hos Proverbs 14:4). “The complacency (šalwāh) of fools” — šalwāh means ease, prosperity, false security — the fool’s fatal contentment with their current state.
33: The closing promise seals the speech: “whoever listens to me will live in security (yiškon-beṭaḥ).” Bāṭaḥ (to be secure, trust) is a covenantal security word: the land promised to the patriarchs is to be inhabited “in security” (Lev Proverbs 26:5; Deut Proverbs 12:10; Jer Proverbs 23:6). “Free from the dread (paḥad) of harm (rāʿāh)” — the paḥad that fell on fools (v26) is absent for wisdom’s followers. Security is the fruit of listening, not the default condition.
